Metadata.Coram: Sundaresh Menon CJ, Andrew Phang Boon Leong JCA, Judith Prakash JCA, Belinda Ang Saw Ean JAD, Chao Hick Tin SJ
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd (in liquidation)

Facts.Text: Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd was a company in Singapore.
Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd was placed into liquidation before the filing of Civil Appeal No 202 of 2020 and was described as being in liquidation in the appeal title.
At the material time in 2020, Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd and Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd were related companies referred to collectively as the Companies.
At the material time in 2020, Lim Chee Meng and Lim Huey Ching were directors of Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, after a company is placed under interim judicial management and then judicial management, its directors retain the authority to cause the company to commence and maintain applications in its name to restrain the company’s retained law firm without approval from the insolvency officeholders.
Party_Details.Rule: Under the Companies Act and the judicial management regime, when a company is placed under interim judicial management or judicial management, the affairs, business, and property of the company are managed by the appointed officeholders. These officeholders exercise all powers and entitlements of directors, including the power to bring or defend legal proceedings in the company's name and to retain legal counsel. Directors retain only narrow residual common law powers limited to challenging or appealing against the very order placing the company into the insolvency process.
Party_Details.Application: Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd was placed under interim judicial management on 2020-04-27 and under judicial management on 2020-08-07. The governing orders stipulated that the insolvency officeholders would manage the company and exercise all directorial powers, including retaining legal counsel. The officeholders subsequently retained Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP to act for the company. In July 2020, directors Lim Chee Meng and Lim Huey Ching caused injunctive applications to be filed in the company’s name to restrain the retained law firm from acting, without seeking or obtaining approval from the interim or permanent judicial managers. These injunctive applications did not challenge the insolvency orders themselves, but instead sought to dictate the choice of legal representation, directly intersecting with the management powers explicitly conferred on the insolvency officeholders.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: Upon the making of interim judicial management and judicial management orders, all management powers conferred on directors—including the power to commence proceedings in the company’s name and to retain or discharge solicitors—are transferred entirely to the insolvency officeholders pursuant to section 227G and the Eleventh Schedule of the Companies Act. Directors retain only narrow residual powers at common law, which are strictly confined to instructing solicitors to oppose a winding up petition or to challenge the specific order appointing the insolvency officeholders. An application seeking to restrain the law firm chosen by the insolvency officeholders does not challenge the insolvency appointment itself and therefore falls outside these residual powers. Recognising a broader residual power for directors to interfere with the officeholders' management decisions would undermine the statutory insolvency scheme. Consequently, the directors lack the authority to cause the company to commence and maintain such injunctive applications, rendering the applications invalid for want of authority. Costs resulting from such unauthorised litigation are to be borne personally by the directors who instigated the actions, rather than burdening the insolvent company's creditors.

Metadata.Coram: Sundaresh Menon CJ, Andrew Phang Boon Leong JCA, Judith Prakash JCA, Belinda Ang Saw Ean JAD, Chao Hick Tin SJ
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd (under Interim Judicial Management)

Facts.Text: At the time of Civil Appeal No 203 of 2020, Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd was described as being under interim judicial management in the appeal title.
At the material time in 2020, Lim Chee Meng and Lim Huey Ching were directors of Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd.
On 2020-05-12, the High Court made an order placing Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd under interim judicial management and appointing interim judicial managers over the company.
With effect from 2020-05-12, the affairs, business and property of Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd were to be managed by the interim judicial managers under the interim judicial management order.

The interim judicial management order for Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd authorised the interim judicial managers to exercise all powers and entitlements of a judicial manager and all powers and entitlements of directors of the company conferred by the Companies Act and the company’s memorandum and articles of association.
After their appointment on 2020-05-12, the interim judicial managers of Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd retained the legal services of Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP to act for the company.
From 2020-05-12, Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP acted as legal counsel for the interim judicial managers of Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd pursuant to a retainer.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether directors of a company under interim judicial management retain the residual authority to initiate legal proceedings in the company's name to restrain the insolvency officeholders' choice of legal counsel without the officeholders' consent.
Party_Details.Rule: The statutory framework of judicial management transfers the authority to manage a company's affairs, business, and property—including the power to commence or defend legal proceedings and to instruct legal counsel—exclusively to the appointed insolvency officeholders. Directors are divested of these powers and retain only a narrow common law right to challenge the order that placed the company into the insolvency process.
Party_Details.Application: Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd was placed under interim judicial management on 2020-05-12 and under judicial management on 2020-08-07. The court orders expressly authorised the insolvency officeholders to exercise all directorial powers, including conducting litigation and retaining legal counsel. The officeholders subsequently retained Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP. In July 2020, directors Lim Chee Meng and Lim Huey Ching initiated injunctive applications in the company’s name to restrain the law firm, acting without the consent of the appointed insolvency officeholders. These applications did not seek to discharge the judicial management orders but rather targeted the officeholders' specific choice of legal representation.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The statutory regime unequivocally transfers the authority to conduct litigation and instruct solicitors to the appointed insolvency officeholders. Directors are stripped of these operational powers and hold only the narrowly defined residual capacity to appeal or challenge the insolvency appointment itself. Injunctive applications designed to disqualify or restrain the legal counsel selected by the insolvency officeholders do not challenge the underlying insolvency appointment and therefore fall strictly outside these residual powers. Consequently, the directors possess no authority to initiate such proceedings in the company's name. The unauthorised applications must be struck out for want of authority. To prevent unfair prejudice to the company's creditors, the real actors behind the unauthorised litigation—the directors—are personally responsible for the resulting costs.

Metadata.Coram: Sundaresh Menon CJ, Andrew Phang Boon Leong JCA, Judith Prakash JCA, Belinda Ang Saw Ean JAD, Chao Hick Tin SJ
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP

Facts.Text: Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP is a Singapore law firm.
On 2020-04-27, interim judicial managers were appointed over Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd.
On 2020-05-12, interim judicial managers were appointed over Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd.
On 2020-04-27, Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd was placed under interim judicial management and interim judicial managers were appointed over it.
On 2020-05-12, Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd was placed under interim judicial management and interim judicial managers were appointed over it.
On 2020-08-07, Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd and Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd were placed under judicial management.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether applications filed in the name of companies under judicial management to restrain their retained law firm are procedurally valid when commenced by the companies' directors without the authorisation of the appointed insolvency officeholders.
Party_Details.Rule: Where a company is under interim judicial management or judicial management, the insolvency officeholders possess the exclusive power to retain and instruct legal counsel and to commence or defend proceedings in the company’s name. Applications brought in the company’s name without the authority of the insolvency officeholders are procedurally defective and subject to being struck out.
Party_Details.Application: Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP was retained as legal counsel by the interim judicial managers of Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd and Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd following their respective appointments in April and May 2020. The firm continued to act for the Companies after judicial management orders were made in August 2020. The insolvency orders explicitly authorised the officeholders to exercise all powers of directors, including conducting litigation. In July 2020, the Companies’ directors filed injunctive applications in the Companies’ names seeking to restrain the firm from acting for the insolvency officeholders, without obtaining approval from the interim or permanent judicial managers. The law firm responded on 2020-10-05 by seeking to strike out these applications on the basis that the directors lacked the requisite authority to commence the litigation.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: Under the judicial management and liquidation framework, the exclusive power to retain and instruct solicitors on behalf of a company rests with the court-appointed insolvency officeholders. Directors do not possess the standing or residual authority to commence legal proceedings in the company's name to dispute the officeholders' choice of legal counsel. Because the injunctive applications seeking to restrain the law firm were filed by the directors without the authorisation of the insolvency officeholders, they are procedurally defective and invalid for want of authority. The law firm remains validly retained by the insolvency officeholders, and the unauthorised applications targeting the firm must be struck out, with costs borne personally by the directors who improperly initiated the actions.
